components.name: Paper 1: Cell Biology; Organisation; Infection and Response; Bioenergetics

commandWords.definition: Determine the value of something from the information given. In some cases, you may need to show working.
Identify similarities and/or differences between two or more things.
Specify the meaning of a term.
Give an account of something without explanations or reasons.
Set out how something will be done. Often used for experimental design.
Use given data or information to obtain an answer.
Assign an approximate value.
Judge from available evidence. Use information supplied, as well as knowledge and understanding, to consider evidence for and against.
Make something clear, or state the reasons for something happening.
Produce an answer from recall or from given information.
Name or otherwise characterise something.
Use evidence from the information supplied to support an answer.
Provide appropriate names on a diagram.
Identify using a recognised technical term.
Write a method for an investigation or practical.
Mark data points on a graph using given information.
Give a plausible outcome based on trends or scientific reasoning.
Draw approximately, showing the key features.
Express clearly and briefly.
Apply knowledge and understanding to a new situation. More than one answer may be acceptable.
Apply given information or a specified technique to answer a question.
Produce a piece of text, or an equation/formula.

questionTypes.name: Multiple choice
questionTypes.description: Select one answer from four options

questionTypes.name: Short answer (1-2 marks)
questionTypes.description: Brief factual recall or simple application

questionTypes.name: Structured question (3-4 marks)
questionTypes.description: Multi-part question building on a scenario or data
questionTypes.markSchemePattern: 1 mark per correct point, may require linked reasoning

questionTypes.name: Calculation
questionTypes.description: Mathematical question requiring working to be shown
questionTypes.markSchemePattern: 1 mark for correct substitution, 1 mark for correct calculation, 1 mark for correct unit

questionTypes.name: Practical-based question
questionTypes.description: Questions about required practicals including method, variables, and analysis
questionTypes.markSchemePattern: Marks for identifying variables, describing method, analysing results, evaluating

questionTypes.name: 6-mark extended response
questionTypes.description: Extended writing requiring detailed, logically structured scientific explanation
questionTypes.markSchemePattern: Level 3 (5-6 marks): detailed, logically structured answer with correct scientific terminology. Level 2 (3-4 marks): mostly correct, some structure, some terminology. Level 1 (1-2 marks): basic, limited detail, little or no structure.

questionTypes.name: Data analysis
questionTypes.description: Interpret graphs, tables, or experimental data
questionTypes.markSchemePattern: Marks for reading data correctly, identifying trends/patterns, drawing conclusions

misconceptionRules.description: Confuses plant and animal cell structures (e.g., thinks animal cells have cell walls or chloroplasts)
triggerPatterns: plant cells don't have a membrane
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Animal cells have a cell membrane, cytoplasm, nucleus, mitochondria, and ribosomes. Plant cells have all of these PLUS a cell wall, chloroplasts (in green parts), and a permanent vacuole.
Mitosis produces 2 identical diploid cells for growth and repair. Meiosis produces 4 genetically different haploid gametes for sexual reproduction.

misconceptionRules.description: Thinks osmosis involves the movement of solute molecules rather than water
triggerPatterns: osmosis is the movement of solute
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Osmosis is the movement of WATER molecules from a dilute solution to a concentrated solution through a partially permeable membrane. The solute molecules do NOT move through the membrane.

Diffusion is PASSIVE — no energy needed, particles move from high to low concentration. Active transport REQUIRES energy from respiration — particles move from low to high concentration (against the gradient).

misconceptionRules.description: Thinks enzymes are 'used up' or 'killed' by high temperature
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Enzymes are NOT alive — they are biological catalysts (proteins). At high temperatures, enzymes are DENATURED: the active site changes shape so the substrate can no longer fit. They are not 'killed' or 'used up'. They can be reused if not denatured.
Antibiotics only work against BACTERIA. They cannot treat viral infections because viruses reproduce inside host cells, and antibiotics cannot target the virus without destroying the host cell.

misconceptionRules.description: Thinks plants only photosynthesise (and don't respire)
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: All living organisms respire, including plants. Plants photosynthesise AND respire. During the day, the rate of photosynthesis usually exceeds the rate of respiration. At night, plants only respire.

misconceptionRules.description: Confuses aerobic and anaerobic respiration products
triggerPatterns: anaerobic produces carbon dioxide in humans
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Aerobic: glucose + oxygen -> carbon dioxide + water (+ energy). Anaerobic in HUMANS: glucose -> lactic acid. Anaerobic in YEAST: glucose -> ethanol + carbon dioxide (fermentation).

misconceptionRules.description: Confuses the roles of insulin and glucagon
triggerPatterns: insulin is made in the liver
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: INSULIN (from beta cells in the pancreas) LOWERS blood glucose by causing cells to take up glucose and the liver to convert glucose to glycogen. GLUCAGON (from alpha cells in the pancreas) RAISES blood glucose by causing the liver to convert glycogen back to glucose.

misconceptionRules.description: Thinks all cells in the body have different DNA
triggerPatterns: skin cells have different genes from brain cells
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Almost all cells in the body contain the SAME DNA (the full genome). Cells are different because different genes are EXPRESSED (switched on) in different cell types. This is cell differentiation, not different DNA.

misconceptionRules.description: Thinks organisms choose to evolve or adapt (Lamarckian thinking)
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Organisms do NOT choose or decide to evolve. Natural selection works because of random variation: individuals with beneficial traits are more likely to survive and reproduce, passing those traits to offspring. Evolution is not directed or intentional.

misconceptionRules.description: Thinks energy is recycled in ecosystems (like materials are)
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: MATERIALS (carbon, water, nitrogen) are recycled in ecosystems. ENERGY is NOT recycled — it flows through the ecosystem and is eventually lost to the surroundings as heat through life processes. Energy enters as light and leaves as heat.
